@graph.headline: Form 1099-DA and Self-Directed IRAs: Why Crypto Investors Need a Qualified Appraisal Before 2026 Tax Season
@graph.description: New IRS Form 1099-DA reporting rules take effect in 2026, creating unprecedented compliance demands for self-directed IRA holders with cryptocurrency. Learn why a USPAP-compliant qualified appraisal is now essential for SDIRA digital asset valuation.

acceptedAnswer.text: Form 1099-DA (Digital Asset Proceeds From Broker Transactions) is a new IRS information return that digital asset brokers must file beginning with the 2025 tax year. It requires brokers to report gross proceeds from cryptocurrency transactions, with cost-basis reporting phased in for subsequent years.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a qualified appraisal for cryptocurrency in my self-directed IRA?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Under IRC §408(i), IRA custodians must report the fair market value (FMV) of all assets annually on Form 5498. With Form 1099-DA creating automated cross-referencing with broker data, a defensible USPAP-compliant qualified appraisal is now essential to substantiate your reported FMV and avoid IRS examination risk.

mainEntity.name: Why can't I just use the exchange price for my SDIRA crypto valuation?

acceptedAnswer.text: Exchange prices are subject to price fragmentation across platforms, liquidity concerns, and market manipulation risks. The IRS's fair market value standard — the price at which property would change hands between a willing buyer and willing seller — requires professional judgment and a defensible methodology, not merely a spot price from a single exchange.

mainEntity.name: What are the penalties for incorrect SDIRA cryptocurrency valuation?

acceptedAnswer.text: Penalties include 20% accuracy-related penalties under IRC §6662 for negligence, potential disqualification of the entire IRA for prohibited transactions under IRC §4975, and up to 75% civil fraud penalties under IRC §6663 for intentional misreporting. With Form 1099-DA automated cross-referencing, the likelihood of detection is significantly increased.

mainEntity.name: What is USPAP and why does it matter for crypto appraisals?

acceptedAnswer.text: USPAP (Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice) is the generally recognized set of standards for professional appraisal practice in the United States. USPAP compliance ensures that the appraiser meets competency and ethics requirements, that the valuation methodology is defensible, and that the resulting appraisal can withstand IRS scrutiny.

@graph.name: Qualified Digital Asset Valuations (QDAV)
@graph.description: USPAP-compliant, IRS-qualified appraisals for cryptocurrencies, NFTs, and complex digital assets for tax, estate, and charitable contribution purposes.

@graph.name: Qualified Digital Asset Appraisal
@graph.description: USPAP-compliant, IRS-qualified appraisals for cryptocurrency, NFTs, and complex digital assets held in self-directed IRAs, for estate tax purposes, and for charitable contribution deductions.
